title: Servo sweep: move to positions
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire a servo's three leads to the board, upload the Arduino sketch, and watch the horn move from 0° to 180° and back — your first part that goes to an exact position on command. Underneath the sweep is the day's real idea: a servo is a small geared motor that reads a repeating pulse and turns its shaft to the angle that pulse's width names, so you command a position and the servo's own electronics get there and hold it.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the servo sweep: a servo's orange signal wire on GPIO 21, its red power wire on 5V, and its brown ground wire on GND, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: Servo
parts.explanation: A servo packs a DC motor, reduction gears, a position sensor, and a control circuit into one case. Send it a timing signal and it rotates its horn to the angle you asked for and holds there — 0 to 180 degrees. Three wires: orange signal, red power, brown ground.

parts.name: 3 jumper wires (M/M)
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: Carries the timed pulse that names the angle.
Feeds the servo's motor with enough current to move.
Gives board and servo the same zero point.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Colour is the map — orange signal to GPIO 21, red to 5V, brown to GND; check that order before anything else.
Land the core idea- a servo is a small geared motor with a control circuit that reads a pulse repeated every 20 ms and turns its shaft to the angle the pulse's width names, then holds there.
Make the Day 22 contrast concrete- with the DC motor the learner set speed and direction and it spun freely; here they hand the servo an angle and its own electronics drive to it and hold.
Point out that this sketch drives the LEDC PWM hardware directly at 50 Hz and 12-bit so the pulse timing is visible; the Arduino Servo library would fold the same maths into write(angle).
If the horn twitches or judders, confirm the red wire sits on the 5V pin with its own jumper — a GPIO cannot supply a servo's current.
If it sweeps once then jams at an end, the horn is hitting something — have the learner refit it on the shaft.
If the board resets mid-sweep, the USB supply is weak — unplug other loads before changing any code.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up, and needs the kit's myservo.py copied to the board alongside the main file.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Push a jumper into each of the servo's three sockets — orange, red, and brown.
Run the orange signal wire to GPIO 21.
Run the red wire to 5V and the brown wire to GND.
Press a horn onto the servo's shaft so you can see it move.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_17.1_Servo_Sweep.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Watch the horn sweep from one end of its travel to the other and back.

arduino.explanation: Sets GPIO 21 switching at 50 Hz with 12-bit precision — the frame rate a servo expects.
Translates degrees into pulse counts — 103 counts for 0° up to 512 counts for 180°.
Sends a pulse of that width every 20 ms; the servo turns until its shaft matches.

micropython.explanation: Builds the kit's servo helper on GPIO 21 — the same pin as the Arduino sketch.
Hands the helper an angle in degrees; it works out the pulse width for you.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: A servo is a small geared motor with a control circuit inside; it reads the width of a pulse repeated every 20 ms and drives its shaft to the matching angle and holds there. The sketch only counts degrees and lets map() set the width.
theoryModel.formula: angle = pulse width — 0.5 ms is 0° · 1.5 ms is 90° · 2.5 ms is 180°

notes.title: You name the destination
notes.body: On Day 22 you set a DC motor's speed and direction and it spun freely for as long as it had power. A servo takes an angle instead — you hand it a position and its own electronics decide how far and how fast to turn, then hold.

notes.title: Why the timing is visible here
notes.body: The Arduino Servo library packages all of this into servo.write(90) and keeps the timing out of sight. This sketch drives the LEDC PWM hardware directly — 50 Hz, 12-bit — so the frame rate and the map() line that becomes the pulse width are yours to read.

notes.title: Where 103 and 512 come from
notes.body: At 50 Hz and 12 bits, each 20 ms frame is 4096 counts — so 103 counts lasts about 0.5 ms and 512 counts about 2.5 ms, the two ends of the servo's travel.

expectedOutputExample: The horn glides smoothly from 0° all the way round to 180°.
It reverses and glides back, then repeats — about 1.8 seconds each way.
The pace is steady, like a metronome.
test.successCriteria: A soft hum while it moves is normal. Each sweep should be one continuous glide, never a twitchy stutter.

firstChecks: Power the servo from the 5V pin with its own jumper — a GPIO can't feed a motor.

troubleshooting.symptom: Sweeps once, then jams at an end
firstChecks: The horn is hitting something — pull it off the shaft and refit it clear.
Check the orange signal wire on GPIO 21, then the red and brown power pair.
The USB supply is weak — unplug other loads from the board.

challenge: Replace the sweep with three explicit commands — 0°, 90°, 180°, holding each for a second — work out each pulse width from the map() line before uploading, then check the horn lands where the maths said.
